title: Distinct Values Queries II
description:
Given an array of n integers, process q queries:
- Type 1: update the value at position k to u
- Type 2: check if every value in range [a, b] is distinct

Input:
- First line: n q
- Second line: x_1 ... x_n
- Next q lines: either "1 k u" or "2 a b"

Output:
- For each query of type 2, print YES if every value in [a,b] is distinct, otherwise NO.

examples.explanation: Initial array [3,2,7,2,8]. In [3,5] all values are distinct. In [2,5] the value 2 repeats. After updating position 2 to 9, the array is [3,9,7,2,8]; now [2,5] is distinct.

mcqChain.questionText: Array: [3, 2, 7, 2, 8] (1-indexed positions). Query: 2 3 5. Should the output be YES or NO?

options.label: YES
NO
It depends on future updates
Cannot determine from given data

mcqChain.explanation: Values in [3,5] are [7,2,8] which are all distinct. So YES.

wrongExplanations.1: NO would require a repeated value in [7,2,8], but there is none.

mcqChain.hint: List the values in positions 3..5 and check duplicates.

mcqChain.questionText: Same array [3, 2, 7, 2, 8]. Query: 2 2 5. What is the answer?

options.label: YES
NO
YES, because 2 appears at positions 2 and 4 which are outside the range (trap)
YES if 1-indexed, NO if 0-indexed

mcqChain.explanation: Range [2,5] is [2,7,2,8]; the value 2 repeats. So NO.

wrongExplanations.0: There is a duplicate: 2 appears twice in the range.
wrongExplanations.2: Positions 2 and 4 are inside [2,5] in 1-based indexing.

mcqChain.hint: Check duplicates within the inclusive range positions 2..5.

mcqChain.questionText: After update query 1 2 9 (set position 2 to 9), the array becomes [3, 9, 7, 2, 8]. Now answer query 2 2 5.

options.label: YES
NO
NO, because 9 > 8 (trap)
Cannot determine without recomputing the entire array (trap)

mcqChain.explanation: Range [2,5] is [9,7,2,8], all distinct. So YES.

wrongExplanations.1: There is no repeated value in [9,7,2,8].
wrongExplanations.2: Comparing magnitudes is irrelevant; only duplicates matter.

mcqChain.hint: Only duplicates matter, not order or size.

mcqChain.questionText: Is any range [a,b] with a == b always answered YES?

options.label: YES, a single value cannot repeat within itself
NO, it depends on the value
NO, unless the value is unique in the entire array
Only if the array has all distinct values globally

mcqChain.explanation: A single element segment cannot contain duplicates within itself.

wrongExplanations.2: Global uniqueness is irrelevant for a range containing one element.

mcqChain.hint: A range with one position contains exactly one element.

mcqChain.questionText: Naively, to answer a type 2 query on [a,b], you could scan the range and use a set to detect repeats. What is the time per such query?

options.label: O(log n)
O(n log n)

mcqChain.explanation: You examine each element in [a,b] once, so linear in range length.

wrongExplanations.1: There is no ordering structure to give logarithmic time without extra preprocessing.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many elements you must check in the worst case.

mcqChain.questionText: Given n,q up to 2⋅10^5 with frequent updates, what per-query/per-update target is reasonable to fit within time?

options.label: Around O(log n) per operation
Around O(√n) per operation
Around O(n) per operation
Around O(n^2) per operation

mcqChain.explanation: O(log n) per op keeps 2e5 operations feasible. Linear per op is too slow.

wrongExplanations.2: O(n) per op would be ~4e10 steps in worst cases.

mcqChain.hint: Think about 2e5 operations and their cost.

mcqChain.questionText: Define next[i] = the smallest index j > i such that a[j] == a[i], or +∞ if none. For [3,2,7,2,8] (1-based positions), what are next for i=1..5?
mcqChain.explanation: Only value 2 repeats at positions 2 and 4, so next[2]=4 and others have no next.

wrongExplanations.1: a[1]=3; its next is not at 2.
wrongExplanations.3: a[4]=2 has no later equal at 5.

mcqChain.hint: Scan each position and find the next equal value.

mcqChain.questionText: Given next[i] as defined, how can we decide if all values in [a,b] are distinct (1-based)?

options.label: Compute min(next[i]) for i in [a,b]; if it > b, then all distinct
Compute max(next[i]) for i in [a,b]; if it < a, then all distinct
Compute sum(next[i] - i); if it > b-a, then all distinct
Check if next[a] > b and next[b] > b only

mcqChain.explanation: A repeat exists iff some index in [a,b] has a next inside [a,b]; so min(next) ≤ b means not distinct.

wrongExplanations.1: max(next) doesn't capture earlier duplicates within [a,b].

mcqChain.hint: Duplicates show up if there is any next inside the range.

mcqChain.questionText: What is an efficient way to compute next[i] for all i initially?

options.label: Scan from right to left, tracking last seen position per value
Scan from left to right, tracking first seen position per value
Use nested loops over all pairs (i,j)
Sort values by index and check neighbors only

mcqChain.explanation: Right-to-left with a map of last positions sets next[i] in O(1) each.

wrongExplanations.1: Left-to-right doesn't give the next position directly.
wrongExplanations.3: Sorting by index keeps original order; doesn't give next equal without extra structure.

mcqChain.hint: When scanning from right, the next equal to a[i] is the last seen at that moment.

mcqChain.questionText: When value at position k changes from old to new, which indices' next values can change?
mcqChain.diagram: … prev_old … k … next_old …   and   … prev_new … (k) … next_new …

options.label: Only k
For the old value: predecessor around k, k, and successor around k; for the new value: predecessor around k and k
All indices with the same old or new value anywhere in the array
Only the successor around k for both old and new values

mcqChain.explanation: Only neighbors among equal values around k (and k itself) can change their next links.

wrongExplanations.0: Neighbors may now skip over k or point to k.
wrongExplanations.2: Far-away non-adjacent occurrences keep their next unchanged.

mcqChain.hint: Only adjacencies among equal values are affected.

mcqChain.questionText: On removing k from the occurrence list of its old value with neighbors prev_old and next_old, how should next[prev_old] be updated?
mcqChain.diagram: prev_old -> k -> next_old becomes prev_old -> next_old

options.label: Set next[prev_old] = next_old if it exists, otherwise to +∞
Leave next[prev_old] unchanged
Set next[prev_old] = k
Set next[prev_old] = next(next_old)

mcqChain.explanation: After removing k, prev_old should jump to next_old, or to no next if none exists.

wrongExplanations.1: It must change to skip over the removed k.
wrongExplanations.3: prev_old should link to the immediate next occurrence, not skip two ahead.

mcqChain.hint: prev_old's next should become the next equal after k.

mcqChain.questionText: On inserting k into the occurrence list of its new value between prev_new and next_new, which next links must be updated?
mcqChain.diagram: prev_new -> next_new becomes prev_new -> k -> next_new

options.label: Set next[prev_new] = k (if prev_new exists) and next[k] = next_new (or +∞ if none)
Set next[next_new] = k and next[k] = prev_new
Only set next[k] = prev_new
Only set next[prev_new] = next_new

mcqChain.explanation: k becomes the next of prev_new, and k’s next becomes next_new.

wrongExplanations.1: next[next_new] does not change; we only connect prev_new -> k and k -> next_new.

mcqChain.hint: k should be placed between prev_new and next_new.

mcqChain.questionText: Which information about a range [a,b] do we need to check to answer "all distinct" quickly?

options.label: The minimum of next[i] over i in [a,b]
The maximum of next[i] over i in [a,b]
The frequency of the most common value in [a,b]
The number of values greater than the median of [a,b]

mcqChain.explanation: If min(next[i]) > b, then no value repeats within [a,b].

wrongExplanations.1: max(next) might be large even if a duplicate exists near the left.
wrongExplanations.2: Computing frequencies per query is too slow.

mcqChain.hint: We only need to know whether a next appears inside the range.

mcqChain.questionText: What should we use to represent "no next occurrence" so comparing against b is easy?

options.label: A very large number (e.g., ≥ n)
The value itself

mcqChain.explanation: Use a sentinel larger than any index so it never lies inside any [a,b].

wrongExplanations.1: -1 would incorrectly appear less than b.

mcqChain.hint: Pick a number that is always outside any valid index range.

mcqChain.questionText: Choose a compact structure that supports setting a value at a position and querying the minimum on any interval.
mcqChain.explanation: This option supports point set updates and range minimum queries correctly in logarithmic time.

wrongExplanations.1: Range queries become O(n); too slow for constraints.
wrongExplanations.3: range_min always returns default; updates also incorrectly propagate.

mcqChain.hint: We need to aggregate minimums over segments efficiently.

mcqChain.questionText: Read input efficiently and set up basic variables.

options.label:
import sys
from bisect import bisect_left

data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()))
it = iter(data)
n = next(it); q = next(it)
arr = [next(it) for _ in range(n)]
INF = 10**18

import sys

data = sys.stdin.read().split()
it = iter(data)
n = int(next(it)); q = int(next(it))
arr = [next(it) for _ in range(n)]
INF = -1

import sys
from bisect import bisect_left

data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()))
q = data[0]; n = data[1]
arr = data[2:2+n]
INF = 10**9

from bisect import bisect_left

data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
idx = 0
n = data[idx]; idx += 1
q = data[idx]; idx += 1
arr = data[idx:idx+q]
INF = 10**18

mcqChain.explanation: This reads all ints, parses n,q, the array, and defines a big INF. bisect_left is imported for later.

wrongExplanations.1: arr remains strings; INF = -1 breaks comparisons with indices.
wrongExplanations.3: arr slices wrong: it should read n values, not q.

mcqChain.hint: Use buffer.read() and map to int to parse fast.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
import sys
from bisect import bisect_left

data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()))
it = iter(data)
n = next(it); q = next(it)
arr = [next(it) for _ in range(n)]
INF = 10**18

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize occurrence lists per value and the next[] array based on them.
mcqChain.explanation: For each value's increasing index list, set next of each element to its successor.

wrongExplanations.1: Setting next to itself is incorrect; it should point to the next occurrence.
wrongExplanations.3: Overwriting lists loses all but the last position.

mcqChain.hint: Each occurrence should point to the next index of the same value.

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize the range-min structure with next[] values.

options.label:
tree = MinRange(n, INF)
# no need to set leaves; they will default to INF

mcqChain.explanation: We must set each position's next value; indices are 0..n-1.

wrongExplanations.1: This uses 1-based indices and will go out of range.
wrongExplanations.3: We need actual next[] values, not all INF.

mcqChain.hint: Iterate i from 0 to n-1 to set values.

mcqChain.questionText: Process queries: implement updates by adjusting neighbors' next links and queries by checking the min in [a-1,b-1].
mcqChain.diagram: On update at k: remove from old value's list and fix predecessor; insert into new value's list and fix predecessor and k.

options.label:
out = []
for _ in range(q):
    t = next(it); a = next(it); b = next(it)
    if t == 1:
        k = a - 1
        u = b
        v_old = arr[k]
        if v_old != u:
            L = occ[v_old]
            pos = bisect_left(L, k)
            prevOld = L[pos-1] if pos > 0 else None
            nextOld = L[pos+1] if pos + 1 < len(L) else None
            L.pop(pos)
            if prevOld is not None:
                newNext = nextOld if nextOld is not None else INF
                nxt[prevOld] = newNext
                tree.set(prevOld, newNext)
            arr[k] = u
            L2 = occ.get(u)
            if L2 is None:
                L2 = []
                occ[u] = L2
            pos2 = bisect_left(L2, k)
            prevNew = L2[pos2-1] if pos2 > 0 else None
            nextNew = L2[pos2] if pos2 < len(L2) else None
            L2.insert(pos2, k)
            if prevNew is not None:
                nxt[prevNew] = k
                tree.set(prevNew, k)
            nxt_k = nextNew if nextNew is not None else INF
            nxt[k] = nxt_k
            tree.set(k, nxt_k)
    else:
        l = a - 1; r = b - 1
        m = tree.range_min(l, r)
        out.append("NO" if m <= r else "YES")

out = []
for _ in range(q):
    t = next(it); a = next(it); b = next(it)
    if t == 1:
        k = a
        u = b
        v_old = arr[k]
        if v_old != u:
            L = occ[v_old]
            pos = bisect_left(L, k)
            prevOld = L[pos-1] if pos > 0 else None
            nextOld = L[pos+1] if pos + 1 < len(L) else None
            L.pop(pos)
            if prevOld is not None:
                nxt[prevOld] = k  # wrong
                tree.set(prevOld, k)
            arr[k] = u
            L2 = occ.get(u, [])
            pos2 = bisect_left(L2, k)
            nextNew = L2[pos2] if pos2 < len(L2) else None
            L2.insert(pos2, k)
            occ[u] = L2
            if nextNew is not None:
                nxt[nextNew] = k
                tree.set(nextNew, k)
            nxt[k] = nextNew if nextNew is not None else INF
            tree.set(k, nxt[k])
    else:
        l = a; r = b
        m = tree.range_min(l, r)
        out.append("NO" if m < r else "YES")

out = []
for _ in range(q):
    t = next(it); a = next(it); b = next(it)
    if t == 1:
        k = a - 1
        u = b
        v_old = arr[k]
        if v_old != u:
            # remove old
            L = occ[v_old]
            pos = bisect_left(L, k)
            L.pop(pos)
            # do not update any nexts
            arr[k] = u
            # insert new
            L2 = occ.get(u)
            if L2 is None:
                L2 = []
                occ[u] = L2
            pos2 = bisect_left(L2, k)
            L2.insert(pos2, k)
            # do not update any nexts
    else:
        l = a - 1; r = b - 1
        out.append("YES")

out = []
for _ in range(q):
    t = next(it); a = next(it); b = next(it)
    if t == 1:
        k = a - 1
        u = b
        v_old = arr[k]
        if v_old != u:
            L = occ[v_old]
            pos = bisect_left(L, k)
            prevOld = L[pos-1] if pos > 0 else None
            L.pop(pos)
            if prevOld is not None:
                nxt[prevOld] = INF
                tree.set(prevOld, INF)
            arr[k] = u
            L2 = occ.get(u)
            if L2 is None:
                L2 = []
                occ[u] = L2
            pos2 = bisect_left(L2, k)
            prevNew = L2[pos2-1] if pos2 > 0 else None
            L2.insert(pos2, k)
            if prevNew is not None:
                nxt[prevNew] = k
                tree.set(prevNew, k)
            nxt[k] = INF
            tree.set(k, INF)
    else:
        l = a - 1; r = b - 1
        m = tree.range_min(l, r)
        out.append("NO" if m < r else "YES")

mcqChain.explanation: This adjusts predecessor links on removal/insertion and updates k and predecessor, then queries by comparing min next to r.

wrongExplanations.1: Mixes indices (no -1), and wrongly updates next[nextNew]; also uses wrong compare m < r.
wrongExplanations.3: On removal, it always sets predecessor to INF even if a successor exists; also compares m < r.

mcqChain.hint: Fix predecessor of old value to its successor; for new value, predecessor points to k and k points to successor.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
out = []
for _ in range(q):
    t = next(it); a = next(it); b = next(it)
    if t == 1:
        k = a - 1
        u = b
        v_old = arr[k]
        if v_old != u:
            L = occ[v_old]
            pos = bisect_left(L, k)
            prevOld = L[pos-1] if pos > 0 else None
            nextOld = L[pos+1] if pos + 1 < len(L) else None
            L.pop(pos)
            if prevOld is not None:
                newNext = nextOld if nextOld is not None else INF
                nxt[prevOld] = newNext
                tree.set(prevOld, newNext)
            # update arr
            arr[k] = u
            L2 = occ.get(u)
            if L2 is None:
                L2 = []
                occ[u] = L2
            pos2 = bisect_left(L2, k)
            prevNew = L2[pos2-1] if pos2 > 0 else None
            nextNew = L2[pos2] if pos2 < len(L2) else None
            L2.insert(pos2, k)
            if prevNew is not None:
                nxt[prevNew] = k
                tree.set(prevNew, k)
            nxt_k = nextNew if nextNew is not None else INF
            nxt[k] = nxt_k
            tree.set(k, nxt_k)
    else:
        l = a - 1; r = b - 1
        m = tree.range_min(l, r)
        out.append("NO" if m <= r else "YES")

mcqChain.questionText: Finally, output all answers for type 2 queries.

options.label: sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
print(out)
for s in out: print(s, end=" ")
sys.stdout.write(" ".join(out))

mcqChain.explanation: Join by newlines to match required output format.

wrongExplanations.1: This prints a Python list representation, not the required format.
wrongExplanations.3: Answers must be on separate lines.

mcqChain.hint: Each answer should be on its own line.

mcqChain.questionText: With array [3,2,7,2,8], next (1-based) is [∞,4,∞,∞,∞]. For query 2 3 5, what is min(next[3..5]) and the answer?

options.label: min = ∞, answer YES
min = 4, answer NO
min = 5, answer NO
min = ∞, answer NO

mcqChain.explanation: min is ∞ which is > 5, so YES.

wrongExplanations.1: 4 is not in next[3..5]; next[2]=4 is outside the query start.

mcqChain.hint: Only indices within [3,5] matter.

mcqChain.questionText: Continuing, for query 2 2 5 on the same array before any update, min(next[2..5]) is what, and the answer?

options.label: min = 4, answer NO
min = ∞, answer YES
min = 5, answer YES
min = 4, answer YES

mcqChain.explanation: min is 4 which ≤ 5, so a duplicate exists in [2,5]; answer NO.

wrongExplanations.1: next[2]=4 is inside [2,5], so not all distinct.

mcqChain.hint: Compare the min to b.

mcqChain.questionText: Assuming neighbor search in a value's occurrence list is done by binary search on a sorted list, what are the costs?

options.label: Type 2 query: ~O(log n). Update: O(log t) to find neighbors, plus shifting cost for list insertion/removal
Both types are O(1)
Both types are O(n)
Type 2 query: O(n). Update: O(n log n)

mcqChain.explanation: Min-range query and point updates are ~log n; neighbor location is via binary search; list insert/remove may shift elements.

wrongExplanations.1: No constant-time guarantees with these operations.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the per-operation costs of the structures used.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the overall extra space used by the structures (excluding input array values)?

options.label: O(n) for next[], O(n) for range structure, and O(n) total across occurrence lists
O(log n)

mcqChain.explanation: next and the tree store O(n) elements; all indices appear once across lists.

wrongExplanations.1: We store arrays and lists proportional to n.

mcqChain.hint: Each index appears in exactly one occurrence list.

mcqChain.questionText: Which edge cases should be double-checked for correctness?

options.label: Ranges of length 1; updates where old value equals new; removing the first/last occurrence of a value
Only arrays with all equal values
Only arrays with all distinct values
Floating-point values in the array

mcqChain.explanation: Boundary neighbors and no-op updates are common pitfalls.

wrongExplanations.1: Other cases can also be problematic.

mcqChain.hint: Think about neighbors not existing and no-ops.

finalCodeSnippets:
import sys
from bisect import bisect_left

data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()))
it = iter(data)
n = next(it); q = next(it)
arr = [next(it) for _ in range(n)]
INF = 10**18

out = []
for _ in range(q):
    t = next(it); a = next(it); b = next(it)
    if t == 1:
        k = a - 1
        u = b
        v_old = arr[k]
        if v_old != u:
            L = occ[v_old]
            pos = bisect_left(L, k)
            prevOld = L[pos-1] if pos > 0 else None
            nextOld = L[pos+1] if pos + 1 < len(L) else None
            L.pop(pos)
            if prevOld is not None:
                newNext = nextOld if nextOld is not None else INF
                nxt[prevOld] = newNext
                tree.set(prevOld, newNext)
            # update arr
            arr[k] = u
            L2 = occ.get(u)
            if L2 is None:
                L2 = []
                occ[u] = L2
            pos2 = bisect_left(L2, k)
            prevNew = L2[pos2-1] if pos2 > 0 else None
            nextNew = L2[pos2] if pos2 < len(L2) else None
            L2.insert(pos2, k)
            if prevNew is not None:
                nxt[prevNew] = k
                tree.set(prevNew, k)
            nxt_k = nextNew if nextNew is not None else INF
            nxt[k] = nxt_k
            tree.set(k, nxt_k)
    else:
        l = a - 1; r = b - 1
        m = tree.range_min(l, r)
        out.append("NO" if m <= r else "YES")

reasoningChain.question: How can we detect duplicates in a range without scanning all elements each time?
reasoningChain.insight: Define next[i] as the next index with the same value; [a,b] is all distinct iff min(next[i] for i in [a,b]) > b.

reasoningChain.question: How do we compute next[i] initially in linear time?
reasoningChain.insight: Build occurrence lists per value and set next of each occurrence to its successor (or scan right-to-left with last seen).

reasoningChain.question: What changes when a value at position k is updated?
reasoningChain.insight: Only links around k among equal values may change: predecessor (old), k, successor (old); and predecessor (new), k.

reasoningChain.question: How do we quickly retrieve predecessors and successors of k among equal values?
reasoningChain.insight: Maintain, for each value, a sorted list of its indices and use binary search to find neighbor positions.

reasoningChain.question: How do we answer range checks quickly once we maintain next[i]?
reasoningChain.insight: Use a structure supporting point updates and range minimum queries to get min(next) on [a,b] fast.

reasoningChain.question: How do we handle boundary cases cleanly?
reasoningChain.insight: Use a sentinel +∞ (e.g., ≥ n) for no next occurrence so comparisons against b work uniformly.
